findings.description: slugify returns an empty string for inputs with no ASCII-decomposable characters (e.g. CJK, Cyrillic, emoji-only text), with no fallback, which can yield empty URL segments downstream.

Invalid characters such as underscores and punctuation are deleted rather than hyphenated, silently merging words (e.g. 'foo_bar' becomes 'foobar'); consistent with the docstring but commonly surprising versus typical slugify behavior.
A negative max_length is not validated and acts as a negative slice, removing characters from the end (slugify('hello world', -1) returns 'hello-worl') instead of limiting length or raising.
When len(suffix) >= limit the result contains none of the original text and the suffix itself may be cut mid-sequence (e.g. '...' truncated to '..'), behavior the docstring does not document.
Truncation slices by code points and can split grapheme clusters (combining marks, emoji ZWJ sequences), producing a visually corrupted character at the cut point.
The 'str | None' annotation requires Python 3.10+ and raises TypeError at import time on 3.9 because the module lacks 'from __future__ import annotations'.
abs(count) == 1 makes -1 take the singular form ('-1 item'), a debatable convention, and non-int values like 1.0 also match despite the int annotation.
mask_char is not validated: an empty string removes any masking indicator entirely and a multi-character string inflates the output beyond the input length, breaking length-preservation assumptions.

The full-mask guard only triggers at len(value) <= 2*visible, so a value of length 2*visible+1 reveals all but one character (e.g. 8 of 9 chars at the default visible=4), contradicting the docstring's stated goal of avoiding leaking most of the content.
The mask preserves the exact length of the value, disclosing the secret's length; fixed-width masking is often preferred for sensitive data.

With visible=0 (allowed by the visible < 0 guard), value[-visible:] is value[-0:] which is the entire string, so the function returns the full mask followed by the complete plaintext, leaking the whole value it was meant to mask.
Negative spaces is not validated (unlike mask_middle's visible check) and silently yields zero indentation instead of raising.
splitlines() treats \x0b, \x0c, \x85, \u2028 and other Unicode boundaries as line breaks and the '\n'.join rewrites them (and CRLF) to bare LF, silently altering line endings and content.
A trailing newline is dropped ('a\n' becomes '  a' with no final newline), unlike textwrap.indent, so the function does not round-trip newline-terminated text.
Docstring says every non-empty line is indented, but whitespace-only lines are non-empty yet left unindented, so docs and behavior disagree at that edge.
O(len(a)*len(b)) time with no input-size guard makes levenshtein a CPU hazard if ever fed long untrusted strings (two 100k-char inputs imply ~10^10 inner-loop steps).

The inner row always spans b regardless of which string is shorter and a fresh list is built per row; iterating rows over the longer string and keeping the shorter as the row would reduce memory and constant factors.
